title: NOTV New Orleans TV — live segment
text: At any rate, we do believe she may have practiced voodoo here herself in the early 1800s. People often ask me about voodoo and voodoo dolls. Voodoo dolls are a tradition that date back to the late 1800s here in New Orleans and they're all made a little bit differently depending on who the voodoo practitioner is. So some of them are made like this with Spanish moss and gregory bags attached. Gregory is another good luck amulet that you will find all over the city of New Orleans and she has the X across her chest which stands for the Quasayan in Haitian voodoo which is where the world of the living meets with the world of the dead. You can find your own voodoo doll here in New Orleans right in the French Quarter in many different locations. I'm here with Vinnie Jones from the Chamet brass band. He just finished a great amazing set of music. I'm from New York. I'm just so amazed by seeing this kind of music for the first time. And I just want to ask you, please explain to me just a little bit about trimmie music and... Oh yeah, well this music come out of the trimmie located around Old Brown Park. And we denigate our show today to one of our... one of the Green's Beedrum players of New Orleans, New Orleans, New Orleans, our new assistant leader, the trimmie brass band. And a bunch of our young brass band have come out of the trimmie. From Bolshevik, Jamel, Rue, Shannon, and Pavel. We come up by a bunch of musicians. I'm Mr. Jam Black, Lloyd Nelson, my father, Chester Jones. We've always been surrounded by Northern music. We got in our blood. And that's great. Now I heard that there's an HBO special that you just did? Yes, we are two-shirt HBO. We do the jazz music, a couple other scenes, you know, and we expect to be in the next scene, too. That's great. Well, thank you so much for your time. And thank you for being a part of this amazing festival that happens every August. And I hope that everyone comes, checks it out. You've got to come down and see it and be a part of this special, special, amazing... I can't even speak because I'm so blown away by how amazing this was and how excited I am, but thank you so much. All right. So here we are at the French Market. Of course, in the French Quarter, it's America's oldest continually operating fresh food market, and that still is the case. And there's a whole lot of seasonality that comes with what you can get. And seasonality has always been a thing with the food here. How we eat, when we eat, and how we prepare it. So we go with season availability things. So crawfish, for example, that's a seasonal dish. You really can only get that late winter all the way through Memorial Day. Pretty much at Memorial Day, hard stop, no more crawfish after that. King cake, that's another seasonal dish. You can start eating king cake on the 12th night, which is the 12th night after Christmas every year. The carnival season starts on the 12th night after Christmas. And that's when king cake starts hitting the shelves, but you have to stop eating king cake on Memorial Day. That's the last day you can have king cake in gumbo. Believe it or not, it is a seasonal dish, although you can get delicious gumbo 365 days a year at many, many restaurants here in New Orleans. But if you're going to be preparing it, if you're going to be cooking it in your own kitchen, generally speaking, it's just a traditional thing. That gumbo is a winter dish. If you get that first nip in the air around October or so, it's all cooking your gumbo. It's all good. And snowballs. Snowballs are a seasonal treat that you can really only get during the summer months. And the snowball stands aren't even going to be open during the winter months. And you can't get snowballs at grocery stores. You have to go to individual snowball stands. Snowballs is a treat, and it just created the best memories of my childhood. I just love snowballs. So that's New Orleans seasonality for the foods. You can come here anytime you get really good food, though. But depending on what time of year you hit here, you might hit the jackpot with one of these. The food world's exciting. I mean, this institute was founded by people in the industry. One of the things for Nokia is to be there to give education to all the men and women that want to be in the hospitality industry. People walk in and they get exposed to Nokia, and they all have the same comment. They go, I thought we already had one of these. It's long overdue, and so many people in this community got behind it to help it become reality. We have two programs. One is baking and pastry arts. The other is a chef program to give you a certificate to give you the skills to get you into a restaurant, get you into a bakery. It's different than what has existed for years. It's a shorter program. You get more skill, more intense, because all the training's done in the lab as opposed to spending time in a classroom. It could be a bartender in Mobile, a line cook in Houston. I mean, we have the two programs right now, but our intentions long-term will be to have ongoing education for professionals in the industry. Those are things that we dream to be able to make available to help this institute continue to grow, and that'll be the evolution. Everybody in this region loves food. They love to cook. They love to learn about it. And I think over the years, Nokia's going to be a real hub for people that come to New Orleans often, want to do something that's food-related, want to get a little more skill, want to get a little more education, and have a damn good time doing it. I think Nokia's going to be the place. Hi, I'm Leah Lefkowitz, a volunteer walking tour guide with the Friends of the Cabildo. So we're here in Jackson Square, the heart of the French Quarter, inside the Cabildo, which is the most historically significant building in the city of New Orleans. Once known as the illustrious Cabildo, it was the site of the City Council under Spanish rule in the late 18th century. By 1803, the transfer of the Louisiana Purchase was actually signed here in this very building. Eventually, the City Council was relocated, and the Cabildo became the home of Louisiana Supreme Court. During that time, the famous case of Plessy v. Ferguson, the appeal of that case was heard here in this building as well. Eventually, the Cabildo went from a place of history to a place that celebrates history. In 1911, it became the home of the Louisiana State Museum, and still today is a museum. The exhibits illustrate early Louisiana history, spanning from Native American through the Civil War. So come visit us at the Cabildo, and the Friends of the Cabildo, a non-profit group which supports the Louisiana State Museum, offers walking tours of the French Quarter every day, twice a day, Tuesday through Sunday. You can go to the 1850 house within the Pantalba buildings and get your tickets there, and you will get a great tour focused on history and culture of New Orleans through the French Quarter. Behind me was once the home site of renowned voodoo queen Marie Lavaux, born here in New Orleans in 1801. She passed away in 1881, actually at the same site where she lived with her children. Voodoo is actually a combination of ancient African spiritual beliefs brought here by the enslaved Africans in the early 1700s, combined with Roman Catholicism, which they were required to practice once they arrived here in Louisiana. So over time, the two religions meshed to form Louisiana voodoo. One of the biggest misconceptions is that people don't realize that voodoo is actually a religion and people seriously practice. It's part of their everyday spirituality, as it has been for a number of centuries here. And you could find practitioners along places like Bayou St. John, Lake Pantatrain, and tucked away in the swamps. You could also find them on occasion, though, out in public, practicing in Congo Square on Sundays, which is located in the historic Armstrong Park just behind the French Quarter. I moved here in 1998 and lived in the French Quarter. And when it came time to buy a house for me and my family and to have everything that I wanted close to the river and close to the French Quarter, I bought my house in Tremay. It was perfect. Tremay has always been the same neighborhood. It was built in 1850, and when you drive around it, it hasn't changed at all. This was America before it was America. This is where the free people of color lived. This is where Congo Square. This is where the slaves were able to gather. Well, if someone's visiting the Tremay, probably the first thing that they're going to want to do is come and eat. So you have places like Fatmas. You got little dizzies on the corner. There's a bunch of places up on Claybourne that you can go grab coffee, you can grab sandwiches. Great place to start your day. Then after that, sure there's going to be a festival around to do some walking in the neighborhood. Check out what it really feels like for a neighborhood that's been around since 1850. There's some great opportunities. Great museums. The Backstreet Museum, if you want to know about Mardi Gras Indians. Humans of all ages and sizes and colors and sexual orientation or whatever you are, you are welcome in the Tremay because that's the kind of community that we are. Hi, my name is Leslie Forenski. I'm a volunteer here at Animal Rescue New Orleans or Arno. Arno is Animal Rescue New Orleans. We are a no kill shelter. We take in neglected, sick animals that no other shelters will take. We help the needy, we help the stray. We are all volunteer based. We get no grants from the government. We rely on donations and we rely on volunteers. We welcome members of the community to come visit us. We welcome tourists to come by. Our hours are 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. 365 days a year and our public hours are 3 to 7. So come on by, visit our animals. We have about 50 dogs and 70 cats in-house waiting for you to come visit. You never know, you may find the love of your life here at Arno Animal Rescue New Orleans. Please visit our website at www.animalrescueneworleans.org and please remember we rely on donations. If you cannot come and visit and visit our animals and give them some social time think about giving a donation online. Thank you. We are an art institution here in New Orleans and have been for over 40 years and our dream is we'll be here another 40 years. And what we do is we teach very classical training. We teach beginning drawing, color and design, landscape, painting, portrait painting. But we teach with a real classical approach. We're beginning to grow into more of abstract art and contemporary art. But at the same time we will always keep our classical training. We have two galleries attached to the academy and they are teaching galleries. We have different artists that come and the students we have over 350 students here each semester. Students get to learn from the galleries and the different exhibits we have. The academy has begun to really open our doors to the community. We have welcomed for one of our shows the Mardi Gras Indians. We have the Tibetan monks doing a San Mandala and we're always looking for interesting opportunities for our students to learn. And we're always inviting visitors and tourists and our local people to come and see our favorite show we do at the academy is the student show. And the student show is always in the end of May right when our last semester ends. And it's a wonderful opportunity to see what the students produce here at the school. So I would like to invite you your friends, your family anyone here in New Orleans to come to the New Orleans Academy show. We're right in the heart of Magazine Street so we welcome you and hope you come in and have a look at the academy. I think you will really enjoy it. Hey Music Lovers, it's David Raleigh with Music Scene here in New Orleans and I am at the iconic Maple Leaf Bar a very very famous bar. If you've been in New Orleans I'm sure you have been here and if you haven't you should be here and I am here excitedly tonight speaking with the owner and manager of that bar, Mr. Hank Stables. Hank, thank you so much for being here tonight. Thanks for coming. This bar is so synonymous with New Orleans and so synonymous with so much talent that comes through this bar that basically there isn't even hardly any questions that I can ask I just want Hank to talk and tell me First of all, how you came to own this great place? How long you've been here? And just tell us a little about the bar. Well, let's see, I was a customer here and I was working at the Bureau of Governmental Research. I lost that job, so I got a job tending bar here. I lived upstairs and they wanted to be sure I could pay the rent. And I worked here for quite a few years and then I was lucky enough to be able to buy into the place. Give me a little history of the kind of music that comes through here, the kind of stuff that's made it famous in New Orleans. Well, I would say that, well, we try to showcase the classic music of New Orleans and I would say that it's soulful music from no matter what neighborhood it comes from, soulful would be the way to describe it. And we've, these days, we're most famous for our brass band connection with Rebirth Brass Band. But really, I would say that our history is more with the piano players than with the brass music. Going back, of course, James Booker considered the best piano player New Orleans ever produced. He played here as a regular act for six years until, well, his last public appearance was here. And Sweet Emma Barrett, Roosevelt Sykes, they were not New Orleans performers, but, you know, Louisiana. So, like, what in your opinion defines New Orleans music? I would say that there's just a soulful sound to the music that people, especially locally, automatically respond to. The music here makes men dance and my experience is that in general, most men do not like to dance, women do and men don't. So, you'll see men dancing and it's a direct response to the soulfulness of the music. So, men, you know where you can dance. Come on down to Maple Leaf Bar, it's a great place. I'm so excited to be here and stick around for some music tonight and thank you so much for supporting my music. Thanks for supporting Maple Leaf Bar. It's great and see you soon. New Orleans is unique. I grew up in North Mississippi, the Mississippi Delta, 2,000 people, a wonderful little Mayberry-type town. I'd say New Orleans is about as far from that as any place you can get. I lived in Oxford, Mississippi, where Ole Miss is four years and I've lived the rest of my life right here and raised my family's been here and raised my kids here. As I said before, unique, different, fun place to live. You get a lot of company. People come visit you when you live in New Orleans, but that's fun to kind of show it off a little bit. I'm very proud of New Orleans, what we've accomplished, especially since Hurricane Katrina. We got our socks knocked off, but it's a great spirit in this town and people fighting back, going through recovery and building our city back. And we've come a long way and got all these great convention facilities and hotels and restaurants and it hadn't dampened people's spirits here. We got a great town, I'm proud to be part of it. Welcome to New Orleans. We're delighted that you have chosen to visit our beautiful city. We believe one of the great cities in America and, in fact, the world. I hope while you're here, you'll take some time to visit our number one attraction, which, of course, is the National World War II Museum. We're telling one of the most important stories in human history through the lens of the American experience. And my guess is you have a family connection to that war through someone that served or participated in the home front. So while you're here in New Orleans, please come see us at the National World War II Museum. Attch into the summer and let the sunshine pay off with three fun scratch-offs from the lottery. Scratch fast, win faster, play quick sevens, win up to $7,000. Unleash the wild side of winning, play wild wind doubler, win up to $20,000. Why wish for luck when you can play double your luck, top prize is a whopping $500,000. Play all three wild summer starters today, Louisiana Lottery, celebrating 35 years of reasons to smile. The Rink has been the center of New Orleans Garden District since 1884, originally built as the country's largest roller skating rink. And now this restored historic shopping center is home to a curated collection of locally owned and operated boutiques, shops, bistro, and bar. The Rink, where locals go and discriminating visitors visit. 2727 Pertania Street at Washington Avenue, just one block from the St. Charles Avenue streetcar. Son of a Saint is a nonprofit organization committed to providing mentorship, education, mental health, and opportunities to young boys who have lost their fathers. Through dedicated mentors, academic support, and enriching experiences, Son of a Saint empowers our boys to overcome barriers and become strong, resilient leaders of tomorrow. But we cannot do it alone. The need for our services in the community is great. And my ask of you is to help us fulfill that need. Through volunteering, becoming a mentor, or donating resources, please visit sonofasaint.org today. Hi, I'm here on Esplanade Avenue. And one historical fact you may not be aware of is that Bayou Road, which is the oldest road in New Orleans, crosses over Esplanade Avenue. And Bayou Road actually starts at the Mississippi River and it comes all the way down here across Esplanade Avenue. It was actually the old pathway that the Native Americans would use to get from the back door of the city, or Bayou St. John, up to the front door of the city, which was the Mississippi River. The Native Americans would actually take their boats, or their P-Rogues, and they would lift them up top their heads, and they would walk along Bayou Road all the way up to the front of the city. Now, the end of Bayou Road would have been called the Manshack, or the back door of New Orleans. Manshack is actually a broken, chop-tall word for back door. Now you know the history of Bayou Road. Music is as much a part of the landscape here as the architecture. Our music reflects the warmth of our people and the flavor of our lives. This is a place known for its music and its musicians, some famous, some not so famous, and surely some soon to become famous. Join singer-songwriter, piano player David Raleigh, New York City's Nat King Cole of a new generation as he explores the local music scene of sleek and trendy, tiny and funky, upscale, the back door hulky tone, and everything in between. We all come to New Orleans to have so much fun here. It's a great city and I'm having a blast. But there's also another side of New Orleans. It's a very giving city. And I'm here with Brian Batt, an actor well-known here in the community. And he's gonna share with us just a few things that while you're in town, you can get involved with to maybe help the community and even help yourself a little bit. Well, there's so many festivals. Besides the fundraisers, there's festivals every time you open the paper. But the fundraisers, I'm involved with a lot of them, especially one I'm very close with, is Art Against AIDS, which happens in the beginning of December. And it's a great party at the museum and also a great art auction. So you can get some really great art at a very competitive price. But there's so many. They're like in the spring there's a zoo to do where you get all dressed up in white dinner jackets or linen suits and go to the zoo and they have all the best restaurants have put out food and they have, you could see the animals in great bands right now. And another in the spring or summer actually is White Linen Night with all the art galleries up and down Julia Street. Another good thing that happens every week in New Orleans, I mean every month, the first Saturday of every month, all the art galleries on magazine and on Julia and in the warehouse district, stay open later for the new installations and everyone has wine and you can stroll up and down magazine or down the galleries and check that out. But I think if people look in the paper or check with the concierge and find out if there's some fundraisers because I like, it's very important to give back and also have a good time at the same time. And it gives you nobody to dress up. David Raleigh was named a child prodigy in the Who's Who book of music. He is a popular recording artist, singer, songwriter and piano player. David's heartfelt songs cross musical boundaries, generational boundaries and the boundaries of six continents. There's more here in Jefferson Parish, a place that offers an unmatched level of accessibility, cultural richness and natural beauty. Where tradition meets innovation, JP is a place where you can be more here. You can thrive more here with companies as diverse as our community. Jefferson Parish is future focused, driving innovation in renewable energy, tech and beyond. We're committed to investing in workforce development and attracting businesses in emerging sectors, all so you can innovate more here. Jefferson Parish is home to some of the most fertile ground for your business's growth. With strong government support, collaborative attraction solution and a focus on both legacy and developing industry, it's easier to create more here. Strategically located at the crossroads of major international river, rail and air hub and spending three significant bodies of water, Jefferson Parish lets you connect more here. And with suburban convenience and a lower cost of living, it's easier to live more here in Jefferson Parish. Enjoy scenic neighborhoods, a thriving culinary scene and endless outdoor adventures, all in a place that keeps you more present, more connected and more inspired. In JP, the future isn't just awaited, it's created. Whether you're seeking a place to grow your business or a community to call home, the Parish offers the perfect balance of opportunity and livability. Discover a place where you can truly be more, here in Jefferson Parish. Well, back in the early 2000s, I produced a television show called, Fugition Justice and we aired it on court TV and we aired it in Louisiana's Louisiana's Most Warned. We worked specifically with the U.S. Martial Service out of Houston in the Gulf Coast Task Force checking down the most elusive fugitives that Houston had to offer. Now what brought me to Jean Lafitte is, my family's been in Louisiana since the early 1700s and of course we've all heard the legend and lore and everything about Jean Lafitte. I mean, every family's got the story, every family's got the quote unquote connection to Jean Lafitte, you know? And so that and plus you can go on the U.S. Martial's website right now and there's actually three pages dedicated to Jean Lafitte. The U.S. Martial at the time was U.S. Martial Deplises and how he could never catch Jean Lafitte. So of course at that time I said, well, challenge accepted and I started researching Jean Lafitte. Now, what was interesting about, particularly my story is there's many legends and theories that exist about Jean Lafitte. You have stories from authors who've written many books on where this guy's really from, where is he from France, is he Spanish, is he French, is he Jewish, is he, nobody really knew. And so the first thing that I needed to do is someone who tracked down fugitives, who is this guy and where was his family from? And the reason that's important to the story is because when you're tracking down the most elusive fugitives in the country, they're always higher than mama's house, grandma's house, somebody's house, but you got to find out who mom and dad are. And so that's what got me into it. So anyway, I've been researching Lafitte for almost 25 years now and I have finally solved the riddle of who Jean Lafitte really was. I have his deptismal certificate, I have his mother and father's marriage contract, I have his grandparent's successions and I can once and for all
